coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen, sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots with large Stokes shift for real-time monitoring of pH in living cells
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xinyi
Wang, Hai
Liu, Qi
Chen, Xiaoqing
coredata.dc:description: Construction of carbon quantum dots-based (CQDs) fluorescent probes for real-time monitoring pH in cells is still unsatisfied. Here, we propose the synthesis of nitrogen, sulfur-doped CQDs (N,S-CQDs) using one-pot hydrothermal treatment, and serve it as fluorescent probes to realize the real-time sensing of intracellular pH. These pH-responsive N,S-CQDs were proved exhibited a diversity of admirable properties, including great photostability, nontoxicity, favorable biocompatibility, and high selectivity. Particularly, due to the doping of nitrogen and sulfur, N,S-CQDs possessed long-wavelength emission and large Stokes Shift (190 nm), which could avoid self-absorption of tissue to realize high contrast and resolution bioimaging. The response of the probes to pH showed a good linear in range of 0.93–7.00 with coefficient of determination of 0.9956. Moreover, with advantages of high signal-to-noise ratio and stability against photobleaching, the as-prepared N,S-CQDs were successfully applied to monitor pH in living cells via bioimaging. All findings suggest that N,S-CQDs have significant potential for practical application for sensing and visualizing pH fluctuation in living systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen
Sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots
pH-sensitive fluorescent probes
Large Stokes shift
Real-time monitoring
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection performance and mechanism of N,S-CQDs toward pH
Reversibility and anti-interference ability of N,S-CQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Innovation-Driven Project of Central South University

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U22A20617 , 22078369 , and 21904141 ) and the Innovation-Driven Project of Central South University ( 2023CXQD048 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM images (inset view) of N,S-CQDs. (b) The size distribution of N,S-CQDs. High resolution XPS of C1s (c), O1s (d), N1s (e), and S2p (f) of N,S-CQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of thionin (i), citric acid monohydrate (ii), and N,S-CQDs (iii). (b) UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of N,S-CQDs.

(a) FL spectra of N,S-CQDs under different pH values from 0.93 to 12.48. The inserted view was N,S-CQDs solutions under a series of pH from 12.48 to 0.93 under UV lamp excitation (from left to right). (b) Plot of fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs vs various pH values. (c) Linear relationship between pH and the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs within the pH range between 0.93 and 7.00. (d) Fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs upon repeatedly switching pH from 0.93 to 7.00. Interference analysis of N,S-CQDs towards various ions (e) and small molecules (f). (λex = 360 nm, λem = 550 nm).

(a) Cell viability of A549 cells treated with different concentrations of N,S-CQDs (incubating for one day). Bioimaging of A549 cells incubated with N,S-CQDs (100 μg mL−1) under varying pH (7.00, 5.00, 4.00, 3.00) of buffer for 12 h. Images were taken in fluorescent channel under 360 nm excitation (b, d, f, h), and merged with both bright field and fluorescence channel (c, e, g, i) (scale bar = 50 μm). (j) Histogram of the normalized fluorescence signals under pH 7.00 (purple), pH 5.00 (pink), pH 4.00 (orange), and pH 3.00 (yellow).
Illustration of design, fabrication, and application of N,S-CQDs.
Comparison of detection performance of N,S-CQDs towards pH with that of reported similar CQDs.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen, sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots with large Stokes shift for real-time monitoring of pH in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, Hunan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, Hunan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Construction of carbon quantum dots-based (CQDs) fluorescent probes for real-time monitoring pH in cells is still unsatisfied. Here, we propose the synthesis of nitrogen, sulfur-doped CQDs (N,S-CQDs) using one-pot hydrothermal treatment, and serve it as fluorescent probes to realize the real-time sensing of intracellular pH. These pH-responsive N,S-CQDs were proved exhibited a diversity of admirable properties, including great photostability, nontoxicity, favorable biocompatibility, and high selectivity. Particularly, due to the doping of nitrogen and sulfur, N,S-CQDs possessed long-wavelength emission and large Stokes Shift (190 nm), which could avoid self-absorption of tissue to realize high contrast and resolution bioimaging. The response of the probes to pH showed a good linear in range of 0.93–7.00 with coefficient of determination of 0.9956. Moreover, with advantages of high signal-to-noise ratio and stability against photobleaching, the as-prepared N,S-CQDs were successfully applied to monitor pH in living cells via bioimaging. All findings suggest that N,S-CQDs have significant potential for practical application for sensing and visualizing pH fluctuation in living systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most fundamental and vital parameters, pH is closely related to human health [1]. Abnormal fluctuation of pH in living cells may continuously escalate dysfunction, leading to increasing damage over time and ultimately resulting in the common diseases, even cancer [2]. Accordingly, real-time detection of pH in living cells can provide critical support for investigating physiological and pathological processes [3]. Unlike the other techniques which need fussy operations, consuming time and high cost, measurement of pH by fluorescence-based method offers numerous advantages including outstanding sensitivity, repeatability, operational simplicity, economic practicality, and noninvasive properties [4–6]. Hitherto, various fluorescent materials such as silicon nanodots [7], semiconductor quantum dots [8], and organic dyes [9] have been designed for pH detection. Nevertheless, they still show some drawbacks such as complex synthesis procedure, fast photo-bleaching rate, short Stokes Shift, low biocompatibility, and high cytotoxicity, which would significantly limit their wide application in biological and biomedical scenarios [10–12]. Consequently, the development of fluorescent probes with excellent optical properties and low toxicity for sensitively monitoring intracellular pH is utterly rewarding.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), ascribed to their simple preparation method, superior stability, nontoxicity and good cell permeability, have been established for bioimaging and detecting pH in biological samples [13]. However, the absorption and photoluminescence range of most reported CQDs is limited to a short-wavelength region and short Stokes shifts, which may lead to self-absorption and significant errors in fluorescence detection due to excitation background interference, ultimately resulting in low bioimaging resolution [14,15]. Unfortunately, research on CQDs that possess both long-wavelength emission and large Stokes Shift, while also being able to monitor pH in living cells, is not widely reported. For example, literature researches reveal that there are only a few examples of the synthesis and application of CQDs with long-wavelength emission, but these red-emitting CQDs do not exhibit any obvious change in pH values [16–19]. Correspondingly, these red-emitting CQDs are insensitive to pH and still need further secondary modification to construct a pH probe [20].

Motivated by the above consideration, we herein report the facile construction of novel nitrogen, sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) by one-step hydrothermal method, and to realize sensitive determination of intracellular pH. As the design, synthesis, and determination process of N,S-CQDs delineated in Scheme 1 , the citric acid and thionin served as precursors to generate N,S-CQDs. The contained nitrogen and sulfur of the precursors could be responsible for more favorable optoelectronic properties and red-shifted absorption of N,S-CQDs, as well as their outstanding response to pH. We found that N,S-CQDs demonstrated a linear pH-sensitive response from 0.93 to 7.00, therefore could be worthy of monitoring intracellular pH fluctuation in acidic condition. More importantly, N,S-CQDs were proved could easily penetrate the cell membrane with low toxicity and high biocompatibility. Therefore, all these properties including outstanding optical stability against photobleaching, large Stokes Shift, long-wavelength emission, excellent biocompatibility, and low toxicity, can offer N,S-CQDs attractive advantages as probes for reversible real-time sensing and visualizing pH fluctuation in living cells.

More details including chemicals, reagents, and instruments, is provided in Supplementary Information (SI, Sections 1 and 2). Different volumes of 0.01 M solutions of citric acid monohydrate as carbon source and thionin as nitrogen and sulfur sources were mixed, and then the mixtures with varying molar ratios of citric acid monohydrate and thionin were treated hydrothermally to form N,S-CQDs. The precursors in ultrapure water (30 mL) were sealed in a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and reacted for several hours (2, 4, 5, 6, 8, and 10 h) under a series of temperature (150, 160, 170, 180, 190, and 200 °C). Then, the pinkish-purple product was filtered through micropore film (0.22 μm) and purified by a 100 Da dialysis membrane. Ultimately, the powder of N,S-CQDs was obtained by freeze drier and 200 μg mL−1 of solution (pH 7.00) was prepared for the next experiments.

Different volumes of 1.0 M H+ and OH− were added to adjust pH values of phosphate buffer by pH meter. Then, for the fluorescent response of N,S-CQDs in different pH buffer, 300 μL 200 μg mL−1 of N,S-CQDs was appended to 2700 μL of phosphate buffer, respectively. After vigorously stirring, each sample was measured under 360 nm excitation wavelength and tested at least 3 times to plot the error bar.

A549 cells (5 × 104 cells per well) were seeded in a 96-well plate and grew for 24 h in DMEM solution with 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % penicillin/streptomycin. Next, the cells were processed with 100 μg mL−1 of N,S-CQDs in DMEM medium, and hatched for another 24 h at 37 °C. Later, after cleaning three times with PBS, the cells were treated with high K+ levels and 10 mM nigericin in DMEM at different pH values for another 15 min. Eventually, all bioimaging images were captured by High Content Analysis System Operetta CLS™ and analyzed by software (Image J).

N,S-CQDs were facilely prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment, in which the precursors thionin and citric acid were directly dehydrated and decarboxylated to form heteroatom-doped CQDs. It was well-established that fluorescent properties of N,S-CQDs strongly depended on the synthesis conditions. Thus, to gain N,S-CQDs with high fluorescence quantum yields (FLQY), we optimized the synthesis conditions as follows. Initially, the products were synthesized with series of different molar ratios of reactants, and their absolute FLQYs were measured after straightforward purification steps. As the results shown in Figs. S1(a and b) and Table S1, with the molar ratio of thionin and citric acid reached 1:0, that was to say, thionin was the only precursor, FLQY of the resulted CQDs reached around 6.1 %. Whereas, the FLQY increased with the addtion of citric acid, and rose to 19.8 % when the molar ratio of thionin and citric acid was 1:2. We conjectured that the additional citric acid would be favorable for dehydration/decarboxylation with unreacted thionin. Therefore, the molar ratio of 1:2 of thionin and citric acid was used to synthesize N,S-CQDs with high and stable fluorescence. With respect to reaction temperature, the products had almost no fluorescence under 365 nm UV light irradiation when the reaction temperature lower than 160 °C. However, with the increase of temperature, the products emitted strong yellow light and reached the highest FLQY at 180 °C. We reasoned that a key to fabricate advanced CQDs materials with favorable structure and optical performance was to alter the reaction temperature, which might influence the condensation and carbonization degree of the precursors in hydrothermal process. Additionally, we also observed that the reaction time longer than 2 h was required for the efficient and steady preparation of CQDs, and 6 h was the most optimized reaction time. Ultimately, the fabrication of yellow fluorescent N,S-CQDs was favorably accomplished by heating thionin and citric acid mixtures with a molar ratio of 1:2 at 180 °C for 6 h.

After the successful synthesis of N,S-CQDs, we then characterized its morphology and crystal structure by adopting transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and fourier transform infrared (FT-IR). As TEM image shown in Fig. 1 a, the monodispersed N,S-CQDs exhibited a uniformly spherical morphology, and then their size was measured and analyzed using Nano Measurer software. As a consequence, the narrow size distribution of N,S-CQDs, ranging from 3.34 to 6.35 nm, was obtained via Gaussian fitting as displayed in Fig. 1b, and their average size was approximately 4.8 nm and consequently well suited to bio-applications [21]. Additionally, the high resolution TEM (HRTEM, inserted in Fig. 1a) reveals legible lattice fringes with a period of 0.24 nm, which belongs to (100) lattice fringes of graphite carbon [22]. The size distribution of the synthesized N,S-CQDs is similar to that of common CQDs reported in previous reports, indicating that a single-size effect is not the reason for emission narrowness and excitation independence [23].

Besides, in order to verify the successful doping of N and S atoms into CQDs, we also employed XPS and FT-IR to verify the chemical constitution and the functional groups on the surface of N,S-CQDs. Four peaks at 284.4, 285.3, 284.9, and 288.4 eV occur in the deconvoluted XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 1c), which are belong to different atomic state of carbon atoms in N,S-CQDs (C–C/CC, O–C, C–S, and OC–N). Moreover, two peaks at 532.4 and 530.8 eV exist in O1s spectrum (Fig. 1d), demonstrating that the strongest stretching binding energy peak belongs to CO and the other peak manifests the presence of C–O [24]. Besides, N1s spectrum can be de-convoluted into two peaks at 399.0 (C–N) and 399.9 eV (N–H) (Fig. 1e) [25], in which the integral area of these two peaks is 49.8 % and 50.2 % respectively. And the S2p core-level peaks appear at 167.6 (S 2p1/2) and 164.0 eV (S 2p3/2), which derives from the dominant C–S–C units (Fig. 1f) [26].

Furthermore, the results of FT-IR characterization can provide more persuasive indication for the element content and functional groups (Fig. 2 a). Two obvious peaks appear at 3443 and 1632 cm−1 in curve (i), corresponding to N–H and CN stretching vibration, can predominantly verify the presence of amino groups [27]. Besides, the peaks observed at 3114, 1608, and 1391 cm−1 in curve (ii) can be respectively attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H, CO, and C–O in carboxyl group of citric acid [6]. Interestingly, the FT-IR spectrum of N,S-CQDs clearly demonstrates the successful doping of nitrogen and sulfur, as evidenced by the prominent peaks corresponding to the stretching vibrations of C–N and CS at 1395 and 1112 cm−1, respectively. This observation indicates a noticeable difference compared to the spectra of the reactants. Besides, the bands centered at 3436, 3111 and 1637 cm−1 stem from N–H, O–H, and CC/CO respectively [28], demonstrating the presence of carboxyl and amine functional groups on the surface of N,S-CQDs [29]. In short, all aforementioned results further shew the successful fabrication of N,S-CQDs.

Then the optical properties of N,S-CQDs were explored by a series of spectroscopy analysis. Fig. 2b shows a shoulder peak at 263 nm, stemming from π–π* (CC) transitions in the core and on the surface of N,S-CQDs [30]. Additionally, fluorescence spectra of N,S-CQDs reveal their excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior [31], which may closely relate with surface states and the size effect [32]. Consequently, due to the trapping of excited state energy on the surface of particles, the strongest emission occurs at 550 nm under 360 nm excitation. Besides, according to the absolute method reported in the previous reference [33], the absolute FLQY of N,S-CQDs can be determined to be 19.8 % which is superior to that of most of reported long wavelength CQDs. Indeed, it is well-established that, doping strategy can alter intrinsic properties of the CQDs by dramatically influencing their chemical and electronic structure [34]. Especially, nitrogen and sulfur co-doping is responsible not only for the increase of FLQY of N,S-CQDs, but also long emission wavelength and large Stokes Shift (Δλ = 190 nm, Fig. 2b) [35]. Furthermore, on a deeper level, nitrogen has an atomic radius close to carbon atom, and sulfur has an electronegativity similar to that of carbon atom [36]. Therefore, the introduction of both of two atoms can change the internal electronic environment, thus result in the stronger photoluminescence as well as shift of emissive wavelength.

Excellent stability of fluorescent probes is a prerequisite for their applications in real biological and environmental samples with the complex matrix. Thus, prior to the application of the fluorescent probes, the optical stability analysis of N,S-CQDs was studied. First of all, their ability for tolerating high salt was evaluated. As expected, when NaCl concentrations change from 0 to 1.0 M, no obvious changes can be observed in the fluorescence intensities of N,S-CQDs (Fig. S2a), indicating N,S-CQDs possess excellent salt-resistance ability. In addition, the stability of pH-sensitive N,S-CQDs was also proved well (Figs. S2b–c) by revealing their minuscule change of intensity under different pH (0.93 and 7.00) and light source (UV excitation and natural light) with time. Advantageously, the excellent photostability of N,S-CQDs was then verified, by which normalized intensity of probes slightly altered after 30 days under sunlight (Fig. S2d). Thus, all findings bespeak that the synthesized pH-sensitive N,S-CQDs with great optical stability and reversibility are appropriate as fantabulous fluorescent probes for monitoring pH in living cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection performance and mechanism of N,S-CQDs toward pH
ce:section.ce:para: Having demonstrated the favorable detection performance of N,S-CQDs toward pH, their detection mechanism was then explored by measuring zeta potentials and fluorescence lifetime of fluorescent probes under different pH conditions. The charge of N,S-CQDs markedly increases accompanied with the increasing pH values, which is related to the protonation and deprotonation of carboxyl and amino functional groups (Fig. S3a). And above results of FT-IR and XPS have proved the presence of active molecular functional groups (–NH2, –COOH, and –CONH–) on the surface of N,S-CQDs, which are common basic/acidic sites for protonation/deprotonation in solutions with varying pH values. Those active molecular functional groups on N,S-CQDs could be protonated and deprotonated under varying pH environments, ultimately lending to the change of fluorescent signal. Likewise, fluorescence decay curves of N,S-CQDs under different pH are an accessible evidence to approve the quenching mode, in which the absorption of N,S-CQDs undergoing collisional quenching are essentially unchanged. The average lifetime of N,S-CQDs solution in pH 7.00 was 3.43 ns, while the average lifetime of N,S-CQDs solution in pH 0.93 reduced enormously (2.68 ns) (Fig. S3b). The results elucidated that the decreased fluorescence intensities of N,S-CQDs in lower pH condition could be ascribed to the changeable protonated/deprotonated degrees of N,S-CQDs, resulting in variable degrees of proton and electron transfer in the internal molecular orbital of N,S-CQDs [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reversibility and anti-interference ability of N,S-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the variable pH changes in a biological environment, the reversibility of the desired probe is highly required for reversible monitoring of changeful pH. As shown in Fig. 3d, N,S-CQDs exhibit favorable reproducibility between pH values of 0.93 and 7.00, even after five cycles. The considerable reversibility of N,S-CQDs based pH detection can be attributed to that the disaggregation-aggregation reversible process exhibits conformity with the reversible changes of fluorescent signals. Besides, it is well known that ingredients in living cells are exceedingly multitudinous, which would bring about a daunting obstruction toward the sensitively monitoring pH value. In particular, the coexistent intercellular substance may have a distinct impact on the fluorescent signal of CQDs, resulting in unexceptional selectivity and low sensitivity. Therefore, anti-interference test of N,S-CQDs towards various ions and molecules that probably exist in cells, was inevitably discussed (Section 5, SI). As depicted in Fig. 3e and f, barely significant variation of fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs is noticed in existence of other ions as well as small molecules, revealing that the interferents can hardly affect the fluorescent signal of N,S-CQDs. Satisfactorily, based on the above results, the probes with extraordinary anti-interference capability, outstanding stability, remarkable detection performance, can be benefit for monitoring pH in living cells.

The outstanding optical properties and pH-responsivity of N,S-CQDs promote us to evaluate their imaging performance for intracellular pH value. First off, the toxicity of N,S-CQDs by CCK-8 test was discussed and the result displayed in Fig. 4 a (Section 6, SI). Satisfyingly, the cell viability rates even maintained above 90 % after being treated for one day with different concentrations of the probes (0.1−100 μg mL−1), suggesting the relatively low cytotoxicity of probes. Then, the pH imaging in living cells with N,S-CQDs was examined. On account of upregulated glycolysis, the high level of metabolic acids and lactate could give rise to dramatical decrease of pH value in microenvironment of cancer cells [43]. Obviously, the probes facilely permeabilized cytomembrane and entered cancer cells with no damage to cell structure under pH 7, verifying fantabulous biocompatibility of N,S-CQDs (Fig. 4b and c). Subsequently, to investigate the applicability of N,S-CQDs for the analytical determination of intracellular pH, different pH buffers were respectively added into the corresponding experimental groups of probes-pretreated A549 cells. As a result, N,S-CQDs emitted a distinctly yellow emitting light under pH 7.00, while the fluorescence gradually weakened and almost disappeared as the pH value decreased (Fig. 4b–i). The software analysis revealed that the calculated normalized intensities under different pH values (pH 7.00, 5.00, 4.00, and 3.00) were calibrated, with corresponding ratios of 1: 0.62: 0.45: 0.31 (Fig. 4j), being consistent with the aforementioned findings. Besides, cells became lightly roundish under the low pH buffer, we could speculate that strong acid environment might decrease cell activity and associate with the diminished metabolic functions. In summary, all findings designate that N,S-CQDs with low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility exhibit superior capability for real-time determination of intracellular pH change and possess substantial application to biomedical research.

To sum up, we designed and successfully synthesized fluorescent probes (N,S-CQDs) with large Stokes Shift for real-time detection of intracellular pH. The yellow fluorescent probes with reversible and sensitive response towards pH can be prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment of thionin and citric acid, endowing the proposed N,S-CQDs with excellent reversibility and sensitivity. Moreover, due to outstanding stability and low toxicity, N,S-CQDs can be potent tools for cell imaging. Significantly, on the premise of the large Stokes Shift of the probes, the fluorescence signal can effectively avoid strong self-absorption from the sample itself, thereby enhancing contrast and resolution in bioimaging. We believe that these pH-responsive probes will have wide applications in biomedical sciences.

Xinyi Zhao: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Hai Wang: Experiments and data processing. Qi Liu: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xiaoqing Chen: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U22A20617, 22078369, and 21904141) and the Innovation-Driven Project of Central South University (2023CXQD048).
